headlines.geopolitics: Navy veteran with no known family buried with full military honors as community refuses to let him be buried alone
headlines.economy: DOJ launches new fraud division targeting 'insane' levels of federal program abuse
headlines.domestic: New poll shows 87% expect AI to transform American life, but most don't find it useful yet

metadata.daily_do: Check on an elderly neighbor or veteran in your community this week

clips.caption: When Duke gets unexpectedly emotional about military service
Pringle drops historical knowledge about the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier
Jax discovers the most ridiculous government AI contract ever

script.text: Look, I've been to too many funerals where three people showed up. But when hundreds of strangers come out for a guy they never met? That's not just nice. That's America.
Good morning, I'm Leo Barros. It's Monday, March 10th. Here's what happened while you were sleeping off your weekend.

Geopolitics first. A Navy veteran named Lonnie Wayman died with no known family in Tennessee. Officials marked his file as 'unclaimed.' But when word got out, hundreds of people showed up to his funeral yesterday morning.
Oh man.

Full military honors. Veterans groups, community members, people who'd never heard his name filled the sanctuary and stood outside. A VA rep said the word 'unclaimed' was wrong — the military community had claimed him.
That's beautiful.
Economy. Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche announced a new DOJ fraud division targeting what he calls 'insane' levels of Medicaid and federal program fraud.
He cited two Pennsylvania men who got four million dollars in federal funds for fake homeless shelters in Minneapolis. The new division will prosecute any fraud over a thousand dollars as a felony.
About time.
Domestic. A new Fox News poll shows eighty-seven percent of voters expect AI to significantly change American life, but only forty-seven percent find it useful in daily life right now.
Eighty-nine percent want disclosure requirements for AI-generated content. Eighty percent are concerned AI is eroding trust in news and social media.
I mean, yeah. Have you seen the deepfakes of me on TikTok? I look way better in them.
There are deepfakes of you?
Not yet, but I'm manifesting it.
And in a fun bonus finding, seventy-four percent of Americans believe intelligent life exists somewhere else in the universe.
Have they met Congress?
Boom! Duke with the drive-by!
Alright, let's dig into that Tennessee story because it says something about who we are.

Lonnie Wayman served in the Navy. He died alone. No family came forward. His file got marked 'unclaimed veteran.' But when the Middle Tennessee State Veterans Cemetery announced his burial, hundreds showed up.
The sanctuary was packed. People stood outside. They released doves. A VA representative said the word 'unclaimed' was wrong — that the military community had claimed him.
This is America at its absolute best. Like, this is what we say we are when we talk about community and service.
Damn right it is.
But here's what kills me — where was this community when he was alive? I mean, I'm glad they showed up, but—
script.direction: trying to balance appreciation with criticism

script.text: Hold on, hold on. You don't know what his life was like. Maybe he was private. Maybe he didn't want help.
Or maybe our systems for taking care of veterans are so broken that people fall through the cracks and we only notice when they're dead.
What do we actually know about veteran homelessness and isolation?
Thirty-seven thousand veterans experiencing homelessness on any given night. Suicide rates twenty-two percent higher than civilians. Social isolation is a documented crisis.
And you know what helps with that? Community. Like what happened yesterday. Those people didn't just show up for a photo op.
Can I just— this is gonna sound weird, but I teared up reading this story. Like, actually teared up.
Why?
Because it's like— we're all so divided about everything, right? But put out the call that a veteran needs people to show up, and hundreds of strangers just... show up.
That's exactly my point though. We can mobilize for a funeral but not for housing assistance or mental health services or—
Look, I've been to military funerals. I've been to too many of them.
And I've been to funerals where three people showed up because the guy was difficult, or proud, or just... lost touch. But when hundreds of strangers come out for a guy they never met?
That's not just nice. That's America. That's the promise we made when he raised his right hand.
But Duke, the promise was also that we'd take care of him while he was alive.
And maybe we failed at that. I'm not arguing we didn't. But yesterday? Yesterday we didn't fail.
There's something about military service that creates this... permanent bond, right? Even with people you've never met.
It's tribal. In the best way. You serve, you're family. Doesn't matter when, doesn't matter where.
I get that, I do. But can we channel that same energy into making sure the next Lonnie Wayman doesn't die alone in the first place?
What if we just made this a thing? Like, not just for veterans but for anybody who dies alone. Just... show up.
There are actually groups that do this. Volunteer mourners for people with no family.
See? This is why I listen to this show. I learn stuff and then I feel feelings about it.
Okay but seriously, what's the policy solution here? Because warm feelings don't fix veteran homelessness.
Sometimes warm feelings are the policy solution. Sometimes community is the answer, not another government program.
But Duke, community failed him while he was alive. That's why he died alone.
Or maybe he chose to be alone. You don't know. I don't know. But I know that when it mattered, when it was time to honor him, people showed up.
Alright, let's bring in our historian. Professor, this story feels like it connects to something deeper about how we honor the dead.
Indeed it does, Leo. This takes me directly to November 11th, 1921, and the dedication of the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier at Arlington National Cemetery.

After the Great War, communities across America and Europe faced a crisis remarkably similar to Mr. Wayman's situation. Thousands of soldiers had died with no identifiable remains, no way to notify families.
The public response was extraordinary. In Britain, they selected an unknown soldier and buried him in Westminster Abbey. France did the same at the Arc de Triomphe.
But here in America, when they brought the Unknown Soldier to Washington, ninety thousand people attended the ceremony. Ninety thousand. For a man whose name they would never know.
Holy crap, ninety thousand?
The inscription reads 'Here rests in honored glory an American soldier known but to God.' The idea being that anonymity did not diminish honor — it amplified it.
What happened in Tennessee yesterday follows this same impulse. When we cannot honor the individual, we honor the service itself.
Doc, that's beautiful, but the Unknown Soldier died in combat. Lonnie Wayman died of neglect.
Ah, but that's where you're mistaken, my dear. We don't know how Mr. Wayman died. We only know he died alone.
And the community's response suggests they understood something profound — that a life of service deserves honor regardless of how it ends.
That's it exactly. The service matters. Not the circumstances.
In Roman times, they had a phrase — 'Dulce et decorum est pro patria mori.' It is sweet and proper to die for one's country.
But I would argue that Tennessee yesterday demonstrated something equally profound — it is sweet and proper to be honored by one's country, regardless of how one dies.
Professor, you just made me emotional about Latin. That shouldn't be possible.
The Tomb of the Unknown Soldier is guarded twenty-four hours a day, three hundred sixty-five days a year. Rain or shine, guards walk their precise twenty-one steps.
That's what those people in Tennessee did yesterday — they stood guard for a fallen comrade. They walked their twenty-one steps.
Professor, thank you. That's a perfect bridge to our rapid fire segment, where Jax will hopefully not make us cry about anything else.
No promises! Alright, buckle up for the weird stuff. First up — Palantir, the AI company that helps track terrorists, just got a government contract to find office chairs.
I'm sorry, what?
They deployed their military-grade AI weapons system to help government workers locate ergonomic seating solutions. I am not making this up.
We spent how much on that?
Two point seven million dollars. To find chairs. That you can buy on Amazon for ninety-nine bucks.
This ties into our fraud story from earlier.
Speaking of which, Louisiana schools have until Friday to post the Ten Commandments in every classroom. But here's the kicker — nobody can agree on which version.
Catholic version? Protestant? Jewish? Apparently there are like fifteen different translations and they're all arguing about whether it's 'thou shalt not kill' or 'thou shalt not murder.'
Well, the Hebrew is quite clear — 'lo tirtzach' means unlawful killing, not all killing.
See, this is why I love this show. I learn about ancient Hebrew grammar while talking about Louisiana politics.
What happens if they don't comply by Friday?
Unclear! But I'm imagining a very awkward conversation between the state education department and Jesus.
Next up — and this is my favorite — somebody named 'Literally Anybody Else' is officially on the ballot for mayor of a Texas town.
That's honestly refreshing.
Right? Like, at least they're being honest about their qualifications. Their campaign slogan is just 'Why not?'
I mean, have you seen some mayors? Literally anybody else might be an upgrade.
And finally, because Florida never disappoints — a drug dealer locked his customer in a storage unit for months over an unpaid debt.
For months?
Months! Like, this wasn't a quick timeout. This was a whole prison sentence. The guy probably started his own podcast in there.
Florida drug dealers have worse customer service than airlines.
I'm imagining the Yelp review. 'One star. Great product, terrible accommodations. Also, I'm still locked in here. Please send help.'
How did they even catch this?
The storage facility manager heard someone screaming during a routine inspection. Which raises so many questions about their inspection schedule.
Alright, that's enough Florida for one day. Let's wrap up.
Today's Daily Do is inspired by Lonnie Wayman's story. Check on an elderly neighbor or veteran in your community this week.
Not because they need you to show up to their funeral, but because they might need you to show up to their life.
A five-minute conversation over the fence. A text asking if they need anything from the store. The kind of small connection that reminds someone they're not invisible.
Leo, that's perfect.
My neighbor Bob brings in my garbage cans when I'm traveling. Seems small, but it's not.
I'm gonna check on Mrs. Chen next door. She makes the best dumplings and I haven't seen her in a while.
As the poet John Donne wrote, 'No man is an island.' But sometimes we need to build the bridge ourselves.
Professor, thank you for that. And thank you for the history lesson about the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier. That really reframed this story for me.
My pleasure, Leo. Sometimes the best way to understand the present is through the lens of the past.
Alright everybody, that's The Friction for Monday, March 10th. I'm Jax Mooney reminding you that if you die alone in Texas, you can still run for mayor.
We'll see you tomorrow.
